title: W5 B Univariate Differentiation
body:
Mathematical Economics

Juan Carlos Carbajal

School of Economics UNSW‚ÄďSydney

Week 5B ‚ÄĒ Term 1, 2026

1/13  Differentiation in R

2/13   Derivative of a Function

Definition 1

The derivative of f : R ‚Üí R at x is the rate of change of f at x for infinitesimally small variations from x: f (x + t) ‚ąí f (x) f ‚Ä≤ (x) = lim . t‚Üí0 t

3/13   Derivative of a Function

Definition 1

The derivative of f : R ‚Üí R at x is the rate of change of f at x for infinitesimally small variations from x: f (x + t) ‚ąí f (x) f ‚Ä≤ (x) = lim . t‚Üí0 t

An alternative interpretation of the derivative of a function is as a linear (affine) approximation to the behaviour of f at x. ‚ó¶ We are interested in approximations to predict changes in the behaviour of the function.

‚ó¶ We focus on linear approximations because the behavior of a linear function is simple to analyze.

3/13   Rate of Change of Affine Functions

Predicting the behavior of a linear or affine function is simple. ‚ó¶ The rate of change of A(x) = m x + b is ‚Ė≥A = m. ‚Ė≥x

4/13   Rate of Change of Affine Functions

Predicting the behavior of a linear or affine function is simple. ‚ó¶ The rate of change of A(x) = m x + b is ‚Ė≥A = m. ‚Ė≥x

‚ó¶ This rate of change is independent of where we measure it.

‚ó¶ Starting at y ‚ąą R, the value of A is: A(y) = m y + b.

‚ó¶ For any t ‚ąą R, the value of A at (y + t) is: A(y + t) = m (y + t) + b.

‚Ė≥ A y+t A(y + t) ‚ąí A(y) mt = = = m. ‚Ė≥x y y+t ‚ąí y t

4/13   Rates of Change of Non-Linear Functions

Let f (x) = 5 ‚ąí (2 ‚ąí x)2 , for x ‚ąą R.

Claim The rate of change of f is not constant. ‚ó¶ Starting at y ‚ąą R, the value of f is: f (y) = 5 ‚ąí (2 ‚ąí y)2 .

‚ó¶ For any t ‚ąą R, the value of f at (y + t) is: f (y + t) = 5 ‚ąí (2 ‚ąí y ‚ąí t)2 .

‚ó¶ The rate of change is

‚Ė≥ f y+t ‚ąí(2 ‚ąí y ‚ąí t)2 + (2 ‚ąí y)2 = ‚Ė≥x y t 2 (2 ‚ąí y) t ‚ąí t2 = . t

5/13   Derivatives as Affine Approximations

Our goal is to approximate the behavior of f around x using simple rates of change. To accomplish it, use affine function that mimics f around x. ‚ó¶ Basic requirement: A(x) = f (x).

‚ó¶ But there are many affine functions that satisfy this.

A1 f A3

A2

x x

6/13   Derivatives as Affine Approximations

Our goal is to approximate the behavior of f around x using simple rates of change. To accomplish it, use affine function that mimics f around x. ‚ó¶ To find the best approximation, define an affine function

A(u) = m u + (f (x) ‚ąí m x), for u ‚ąą R.

‚ó¶ Note that A(x) = f (x) as desired.

‚ó¶ Note also that A(u) ‚ąí A(x) = m (u ‚ąí x) = T (u ‚ąí x).

‚ó¶ To find the best affine approximation we would require that the error |A(u) ‚ąí f (u)| ‚Üí 0 faster than |u ‚ąí x| ‚Üí 0.

‚ó¶ This is equivalent to requiring that   A(u) ‚ąí A(x) ‚ąí f (u) ‚ąí f (x) ‚Üí 0

faster than |u ‚ąí x| ‚Üí 0.

7/13

‚ó¶ Since | ‚ąí z| = |z|, we can write the above requirement as   f (u) ‚ąí f (x) ‚ąí A(u) ‚ąí A(x) = |f (u) ‚ąí f (x) ‚ąí T (u ‚ąí x)| .

‚ó¶ To find the best linear approximation, verify that the expression

|f (u) ‚ąí f (x) ‚ąí T (u ‚ąí x)| |u ‚ąí x|

converges to 0 as u ‚Üí x.

8/13

Definition 2

Let I be an open interval and f : I ‚Üí R. Say that f is differentiable at x ‚ąą I if there is a linear mapping T : R ‚Üí R such that f (u) ‚ąí f (x) ‚ąí T (u ‚ąí x) lim = 0. u‚Üíx u‚ąíx

In this case, the linear mapping T is called the derivative of f at x and denoted by T = Df (x).

9/13

Definition 2

Let I be an open interval and f : I ‚Üí R. Say that f is differentiable at x ‚ąą I if there is a linear mapping T : R ‚Üí R such that f (u) ‚ąí f (x) ‚ąí T (u ‚ąí x) lim = 0. u‚Üíx u‚ąíx

In this case, the linear mapping T is called the derivative of f at x and denoted by T = Df (x).

Observe Definition 2 is equivalent to

f (u) ‚ąí f (x) lim = Df (x). u‚Üíx u‚ąíx

9/13

Example 1

The derivative of a constant function c(x) = őļ is Dc(x) = 0: |c(u) ‚ąí c(x) ‚ąí T (u ‚ąí x)| |c(u) ‚ąí c(x) ‚ąí Dc(x)(u ‚ąí x)| u‚Üíx |u ‚ąí x| u‚Üíx |u ‚ąí x|

The derivative of f (x) = x is Df (x) = 1: |f (u) ‚ąí f (x) ‚ąí T (u ‚ąí x)| |f (u) ‚ąí f (x) ‚ąí Df (x)(u ‚ąí x) lim = lim u‚Üíx |u ‚ąí x| u‚Üíx u ‚ąí x |u ‚ąí x ‚ąí 1 (u ‚ąí x)| u‚Üíx |u ‚ąí x|

Using an induction argument, one can show that the derivative of f (x) = xn is Df (x) = nxn‚ąí1 .

Example 4

The derivative of f (x) = 5 ‚ąí (2 ‚ąí x)2 at y ‚ąą R is Df (y) = 4 ‚ąí 2y.

13/13

Example 4

The derivative of f (x) = 5 ‚ąí (2 ‚ąí x)2 at y ‚ąą R is Df (y) = 4 ‚ąí 2y.

Show Following the definition of the derivative of f at y obtains

f (y + t) ‚ąí f (y) ‚ąí(2 ‚ąí y ‚ąí t)2 + (2 ‚ąí y)2 lim = lim t‚Üí0 t t‚Üí0 t 2 (2 ‚ąí y) t ‚ąí t2 t‚Üí0 t
